coredata.dc:title: 3D origami paper-based ratiometric fluorescent microfluidic device for visual point-of-care detection of alkaline phosphatase and butyrylcholinesterase
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yongfeng
Tong, Xia
Wei, Qisheng
Cai, Guihan
Cao, Yuanxin
Tong, Chaoying
Shi, Shuyun
Wang, Fang
coredata.dc:description: On-site multiplex enzyme detection is crucial for diagnosis, therapeutics and prognostic. To date, it is still a daunting challenge to develop portable, low-cost, and efficient multi-enzyme detection methods. Herein, a novel sample-in-result-out platform integrating ratiometric fluorescent assays with 3D origami microfluidic paper-based device (μPAD) was developed for simultaneous visual point-of-care testing (POCT) of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE). Cascade catalytic reaction with the same two fluorescent signal indicators was rationally designed to ratiometric fluorescent detection of ALP and BChE: substrate of ALP (pyrophosphate) and product of BChE (thiocholine) can strongly complex with Cu2+, Cu2+ oxidizes o-phenylenediamine to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) (emission, 565 nm), oxOPD quenches the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs, emission at 445 nm) via inner filter effect, thus oxOPD/CDs values are relevant to ALP and BChE activities. Then 3D origami μPAD composing of four layers and two parallel channels was fabricated and simply prepared by one-step plotting with black oil-based marker and specific metal molds. After simple folding and unfolding neighboring layers to sequentially initiate reactions of pre-loaded reagents, fluorescent images on the detection zone can be captured by smartphone and analyzed by red-green-blue software for quantitative analysis. Under optimal conditions, the proposed platform was successfully performed to detect ALP and BChE with activity difference at 3 orders of magnitude in human serum samples without any pretreatment procedures. Excellent selectivity, good precision, favorable linear range, and high accuracy were exhibited. Importantly, the platform opens a promising horizon for high-throughput POCT of multiplex biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Origami paper-based microfluidic device
Point-of-care testing
Ratiometric fluorescent detection
Carbon dots
Alkaline phosphatase
Butyrylcholinesterase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and preparation of 3D origami μPAD
Simultaneous detection of ALP and BChE by 3D origami μPAD
Fabrication and fluorescent mechanism of simultaneous assay ALP and BChE activity
Optimization and specificity of 3D origami μPAD-based fluorescent assay
Real human serum sample assay by 3D origami μPAD platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fabrication procedures for 3D μPAD; (b) Photo of 3D origami μPAD taken by smartphone.

(a) Hydrothermal synthetic process of CDs; (b) TEM image (inserted HR-TEM) for CDs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (d) XPS survey spectrum of CDs; (e) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black), fluorescence emission spectrum at 365 nm excitation (red), and fluorescence excitation spectra at 445 nm emissions (blue) for CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effect of ions, anions, amino acids, BSA, and enzymes on (a) ALP and (b) BChE assay; linear calibration plots for (c) ALP and (d) BChE detection. Inserted are the fluorescent images being captured by a smartphone under a 365 nm UV-lamp excitation (ALP, from left to right: 5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 350 U L−1, BChE, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 22.0, 25.0 U mL−1).
Schematic representation of ratiometric fluorescent detection mechanism (a) and smartphone-assisted 3D μPAD (b) for simultaneous visual sensing of ALP and BChE.
Detection of ALP and BChE activities in human serum samples (samples 1 and 2 for people with primary biliary cholangitis, samples 3 and 4 for healthy people) by 3D origami μPAD platform.

head.ce:title: 3D origami paper-based ratiometric fluorescent microfluidic device for visual point-of-care detection of alkaline phosphatase and butyrylcholinesterase

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: On-site multiplex enzyme detection is crucial for diagnosis, therapeutics and prognostic. To date, it is still a daunting challenge to develop portable, low-cost, and efficient multi-enzyme detection methods. Herein, a novel sample-in-result-out platform integrating ratiometric fluorescent assays with 3D origami microfluidic paper-based device (μPAD) was developed for simultaneous visual point-of-care testing (POCT) of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE). Cascade catalytic reaction with the same two fluorescent signal indicators was rationally designed to ratiometric fluorescent detection of ALP and BChE: substrate of ALP (pyrophosphate) and product of BChE (thiocholine) can strongly complex with Cu2+, Cu2+ oxidizes o-phenylenediamine to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) (emission, 565 nm), oxOPD quenches the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs, emission at 445 nm) via inner filter effect, thus oxOPD/CDs values are relevant to ALP and BChE activities. Then 3D origami μPAD composing of four layers and two parallel channels was fabricated and simply prepared by one-step plotting with black oil-based marker and specific metal molds. After simple folding and unfolding neighboring layers to sequentially initiate reactions of pre-loaded reagents, fluorescent images on the detection zone can be captured by smartphone and analyzed by red-green-blue software for quantitative analysis. Under optimal conditions, the proposed platform was successfully performed to detect ALP and BChE with activity difference at 3 orders of magnitude in human serum samples without any pretreatment procedures. Excellent selectivity, good precision, favorable linear range, and high accuracy were exhibited. Importantly, the platform opens a promising horizon for high-throughput POCT of multiplex biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:para: Point-of-care testing (POCT), an important branch of in vitro diagnostics, has been developed rapidly in modern analytical chemistry and clinical testing (Zhou et al., 2021). POCT has irreplaceable advantages of simple operation and convenient set-up, providing the possibility of rapid on-site detection especially for family health care, and preliminary diagnosis of diseases in remote areas (Ventura et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019). Microfluidic paper-based device (μPAD) concentrates multiple experimental steps on the hydrophilic detection zones of a tiny piece of paper, which possesses many attractive features in developing simple, portable, disposable, low-cost, and rapid on-site diagnostic tools for POCT (Caratelli et al., 2020; Chinnadayyala et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2017; Verma et al., 2018). Moreover, μPAD allows for the consumption of small volumes of sample and reagent (Kim et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2017). To date, a series of creative fabrication methods for μPAD have been reported, such as photolithography, wax printing, inkjet printing, laser toner printing, PDMS printing, drawing, etc. (Xia et al., 2016). Notably, commercially available permanent markers have been recently used to facilely plot μPAD within 1 min (Nie et al., 2012). Then, combining with appropriate detection methods (e.g. electrochemistry, colorimetry, fluorescence, luminescence), various 2D and 3D μPADs have been successfully developed and widely applied in bioanalysis, medical diagnosis, and environmental monitoring (Ding et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Tenda et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2019). For example, Tenda et al. reported 2D μPAD, which relies on bioluminescence resonance energy transfer switching for colorimetric signal generation and proteins recognition (Tenda et al., 2018). Cinti et al. fabricated 2D μPAD to detect ethanol in beer samples by electrochemical methods (Cinti et al., 2017). Trieu et al. reported 3D origami μPAD for colorimetric identification of live cells (Trieu et al., 2019). Some textile-based and thread-based microfluidic devices have been introduced recently (Choi et al., 2018; Tomimuro et al., 2020), which requires less sample volume and shorter analysis time by comparison with μPAD. It is worthy mentioned that the relatively better sensitivity of μPAD has facilitated its wide application. In recent years, as an instrument-free analytical devices, smartphone has attracted particular attentions by its high-resolution visual imaging of colorimetric and fluorometric results for accurate quantification (Kou et al., 2020), which will open broad perspectives for μPAD-based POCT. Up to now, developing a novel μPAD for facile, efficient, accurate, and simultaneous detection of multiple analytes is still highly desirable.

Enzymes are widely present in biological organisms and participate in the regulation of biological processes, while abnormal enzyme activity is always related to some diseases (Dong et al., 2020; Tong et al., 2020b). Serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) are clinically considered as two important biomarkers for the diagnosis of liver diseases, such as liver dysfunction, liver cirrhosis and chronic hepatitis (Chen et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2020; Phoonsawat et al., 2021). Additionally, abnormal expressions of ALP and BChE are associated with hyperthyroidism, diabetes, high blood pressure, cancer, etc (Klocker et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020). As a result, efficient, convenient, sensitive, and simultaneous detection of serum ALP and BChE activity is a crucial need for early diagnosis and prognostic of related diseases. Currently, many efforts have been made to develop methods for ALP or BChE detection, such as fluorometric (Tong et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020b), colorimetric (Chen et al., 2020), electrochemical (Shen et al., 2016), and Raman spectroscopy (Nechaeva et al., 2018) assays. Among them, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent methods have been predominantly fabricated owning to their superior reliability, excellent sensitivity, and high selectivity, and we have reviewed these strategies in detail (Tong et al., 2020b). However, an enormous challenge is still encountered for simultaneously monitoring ALP and BChE because of the difficulty in discrimination of two enzymatic reactions.

By comparison with single fluorescence signal, ratiometric fluorescent method has attracted a great deal of attentions in on-site sensing due to the offset performance variation, enhanced color change resolution and improved quantitative accuracy (Gui et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2021). However, there still remains obstacles in ratiometric sensing of multiple analytes using μPAD, such as lack of appropriate reaction and detection systems, and potential concerns of signal transduction (Fu et al., 2018; Srisomwat et al., 2020). Herein, a robust, portable, and practical 3D origami paper-based ratiometric fluorescent microfluidic device was fabricated for simultaneous detection of ALP and BChE using cascade catalytic reaction with the same two fluorescent signal indicators. As showcased in Scheme 1 A, pyrophosphate (PPi, enzymatic substrate of ALP) and thiocholine (TCh, enzymatic product of BChE) can coordinate with Cu2+, free Cu2+ oxidizes o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD, Abs. maximum at 420 nm, and Em. maximum at 565 nm), and then oxOPD quenches the emission of CDs (Em. maximum at 445 nm) via inner filter effect (IFE). Thus, ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform with dual emissions at 445 nm (blue fluorescence from CDs) and 565 nm (yellow fluorescence from oxOPD) were successfully developed for ALP and BChE sensing. The addition of ALP facilitates the production of oxOPD, and the emission of CDs decreases. Whereas for BChE, opposite results were observed. Based on the sensing mechanism, a 3D origami μPAD has been developed, which possess four layers with two parallel hydrophilic channels to realized simultaneous detection of ALP and BChE (Scheme 1B). Each layer features pre-stored reagents for better detection. ALP/BChE and substrate react in the 1st layer, then remaining substrate and enzymatic product move to the 2nd layer to bind with Cu2+. Once unreacted Cu2+ reaches the 3rd layer and reacts with pre-stored OPD, oxOPD occurs and shifts to the 4th layer to quench the fluorescence of CDs. The fluorescent images of 4th layer are captured by smartphone and analyzed by red-green-blue (RGB) software for visual quantitative analysis. Thus, a fully integrated sample-in-result-out system has been developed. The developed 3D origami μPAD has been successfully employed to simultaneously detect ALP and BChE in real serum samples for POCT. The 3D origami μPAD possesses attractive characteristics, such as low cost, simple operation, high efficiency, good accuracy, excellent sensitivity, and desirable selectivity, which may provide an innovative way in POCT field.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of eggplant. Typically, eggplants (25.0 g) and ultrapure water (15.0 mL) were mixed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at temperature of 190 °C for 10 h. The obtained dark products were then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and a brown filter solution was collected. Subsequently, the required solution was subjected to dialysis with a cellulose ester membrane bag (molecular-weight cutoff, 500) for two days. After that, the obtained yellow solution was further filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and freeze-dried. Finally, the obtained CDs were re-dispersed in ultrapure water with a concentration at 10 mg mL−1 for further operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and preparation of 3D origami μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: Non-fluorescent Whatman no. 4 filter paper with a square of 63 mm × 100 mm size was selected as the substrate to fabricate a 3D origami μPAD, which was folded into four-layer flow passage with a rectangle of 63 mm × 25 mm size (i.e. sample layer, reaction layers, and detection layer) (Fig. 1 ). Metal molds with specific pattern and size were placed on the paper, and then a commercially obtained black oil-based marker was selected to directly plot the pattern of the mold to construct hydrophobic hollow barrier. The 1st layer contained a central sample zone (diameter, 8 mm) and I-shaped path (width, 3 mm; length, 9 mm) to split sample into two equal reaction/detection channels (A and B, diameter, 6 mm) for simultaneous sensing ALP and BChE. Reaction/detection zones of four unfolded layers of channel A were pre-deposited with PPi (5 μL, 5 mM, containing 0.1 mM Mg2+), Cu2+ (5 μL, 0.6 mM), OPD (5 μL, 0.1 M), and CDs (5 μL, 3 mg mL−1), successively. While for channel B, butyrylthiocholine (BTCh, 5 μL, 0.1 M), Cu2+ (5 μL, 4 mM), OPD (5 μL, 0.1 M), and CDs (5 μL, 10 mg mL−1) were pre-loaded. Finally, each prepared 3D origami μPAD was exposed to air at the room temperature to completely dry, stored in a sealed bag at 4 °C, and kept away from light. No significant loss of sensitivity was found during the storage period of one month.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of ALP and BChE by 3D origami μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: ALP and BChE standard solutions with different concentrations or real human serum samples (20 μL) were added dropwise to the sample zone on the 1st layer, and placed in an incubator with humid environment at 37 °C for incubation for 30 min. Then the 2nd layer was folded onto the 1st layer and Tris-HCl buffer (5 μL, 10 mM, pH 8.0) was dropped to the reaction zone on the 1st layer to bring the substate and enzymatic product to the 2nd layer for another 10 min incubation at 37 °C. After that, the 1st layer was unfolded, the 3rd layer was folded onto the 2nd layer, and another Tris-HCl buffer (5 μL, 10 mM, pH 8.0) was dispensed and allowed free Cu2+ to react with OPD at 37 °C for 25 min. Finally, the 4th layer was folded to the 3rd layer, and fluorescent images were immediately captured by smartphone under a handheld UV-lamp (Ex at 365 min). The software (Color Recognition Application) installed on the smartphone can output the corresponding RGB value of the fluorescence image. The ALP was quantitatively analyzed based on the G/B (the ratio of G to B) value on the 4th layer, and BChE based on the (R + G + B)/R (the ratio of the sum of RGB to R) value on the 4th layer. Note that the 3D origami μPAD realized multi-step orderly reactions by folding, and each layer allowed two independent reactions without crosstalk.

Natural biomasses have been proven to be good starting materials for synthesis of CDs because of their easy-obtained, low-cost, and environmentally friendly properties (Tong et al., 2021). Eggplant has been reported to be rich of phenylpropanoid amides, phenolic acids, and carbohydrates (Song et al., 2021), which can provide carbon and nitrogen source for CDs. As a result, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of eggplant, which included extraction of molecules, polymerization, and carbonization (Fig. 2 a). In order to obtain CDs with the maximum sensitivity, preparation conditions (i.e. mass of eggplant, reaction time, and reaction temperature) were optimized (Fig. S1). It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs reaches the maximum when eggplant (25 g) and water (15 mL) are mixed in a Teflon-lined autoclave to react at 190 °C for 10 h.

A series of characterizations were performed to confirm the successful synthesis of CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 2b and Fig. S2a that CDs are monodisperse with an average diameter of about 5 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image inserted in Fig. 2b presents that the lattice distance of CDs is 0.21 nm, attributing to the (100) plane of graphite carbons. In X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S2b), CDs have a broad peak at 21.5°, indicating that they are amorphous carbon phase (Tong et al., 2021). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs shows that CDs have absorption peaks at 3225, 2931, 1766, 1578, 1351, 1277 and 1070 cm−1 (Fig. 2c), which belong to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, C–H, CO, CC, C–N, C–O, C–O–C (Tong et al., 2021). Thus, it can be inferred that there contains –COOH, –OH, –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs. This conclusion can be verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of CDs. In full XPS survey spectrum, CDs present O1s (531.61 eV), N1s (399.71 eV), and C1s (284.81 eV) peaks with relative elemental contents of C, N, and O at 66.75%, 8.25%, and 25.00% (Fig. 2d). In C 1s spectrum, peaks at 284.62 eV (CC/C–C), 285.40 eV (C–N), 286.20 eV (C–O), and 287.80 eV (CO/CN) are exhibited (Fig. S3a). Whereas for O 1s spectrum, 531.30 eV (CO) and 532.40 eV (C–O) are observed (Fig. S3b). And for N 1s spectrum, 399.50 eV (pyrrolic–N) and 400.00 eV (graphitic–N) are presented (Fig. S3c). Obviously, CDs with –COOH, –OH, –NH2 groups on the surface have been successfully synthesized.

UV–Vis spectrum shows that CDs have absorption bands at 245 and 320 nm (Fig. 2e), which are attributed to π→π* (CC) and n→π* (CN and CO) electronic transitions. Fluorescent spectrum shows the maximum emission peak of CDs at 445 nm under excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 2d). When the excitation wavelengths increase from 325 to 395 nm, the emission peak of CDs is red-shifted (Fig. S4), indicating the excitation-dependent behavior of CDs, which is an iconic feature of CDs (Tong et al., 2021). The quantum yield of CDs using quinine sulfate as reference is calculated as 3.65%.

In biosensing, the optical stability of CDs is significantly important. Thus, the effect of pH, ionic strength and light irradiation under 365 nm were investigated. As showcased in Fig. S5, the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs can be ignored under pH from 7.0 to 9.0, concentration of NaCl from 0 to 800 mM, and continuous UV irradiation for 90 min. Thus, the prepared CDs show excellent optical stability, and then can be performed as a superior nano-sensor for biosensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and fluorescent mechanism of simultaneous assay ALP and BChE activity
ce:section.ce:para: For enzymatic activity assay, fluorescent methods have attracted great attentions because of their simple operation, excellent sensitivity, high accuracy, and superior biocompatibility (Tong et al., 2020b). However, to date, reports are focused on the single-enzyme assay approaches, and it is still a challenge to simultaneous sensing of multiple enzymes (Tong et al., 2020). Interestingly, PPi (enzymatic substrate of ALP) and TCh (enzymatic product of BChE) can complex with Cu2+, while phosphate (Pi, enzymatic product of ALP) and butyrylthiocholine (BTCh, enzymatic substrate of BChE) do not react with Cu2+. Subsequently, Cu2+ can oxidize OPD into oxOPD, a yellow fluorescent compound with maximum emission at 565 nm (Fig. S6a). As a result, cascade catalytic reaction system, PPi (BTCh)/Cu2+/OPD, can be developed for ALP (BChE) detection. More significantly, ratiometric fluorescent methods with high accuracy are mostly desired owning to the rid of the effect of background interference and surrounding environment (Long et al., 2020). Thinking of the absorption spectrum (Abs. maximum at 420 nm) of oxOPD, oxOPD can quench the fluorescence of blue-emission CDs via IFE. As illustrated in Fig. S6a, UV–vis absorption spectrum of oxOPD has a good overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of the prepared CDs. At the same time, fluorescence lifetime of CDs seldom changes after addition of oxOPD (Fig. S6b), indicating the IFE between CDs and oxOPD. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescent system using the same two fluorescent signal indicators (oxOPD and CDs), can be fabricated to response ALP (BChE) accurately, sensitively, and selectively (Scheme 1a). The asynchronous fluorescent change of CDs and oxOPD is in close correlation to ALP (BChE) activity. Here, in order to realize visual detection, 365 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength. As presented in Fig. S7a, with the increase of ALP activity, the consumption of PPi prompts the production of more oxOPD, which effectively decreases the fluorescence emission of CDs. For BChE, the opposite results are observed (Fig. S7b).

In order to on-site sense ALP and BChE simultaneously, 3D origami μPAD with four layer and two parallel channels was designed according to the working principle (Fig. S8). Reagents (PPi/BTCh, Cu2+, OPD, CDs) have been successively preloaded on the layer, and as a result, reactions can sequentially happen according to the flow of the sample solution without any crosstalk after folding two neighboring layers. For channel A, after addition of sample, ALP decomposes PPi to produce Pi (1st layer), unreacted PPi complexes with Cu2+ (2nd layer), remained Cu2+ reacts with OPD to form oxOPD (3rd layer), and oxOPD mixes with CDs for ratiometric detection. For channel B, BTCh in sample catalyzes BTCh into TCh (1st layer), produced TCh complexes with Cu2+ (2nd layer), and the left two procedures are the same with those of channel A. The fluorescent images of detection zones can be simply captured by smartphone. Thus, a simple, efficient, portable, and robust POCT platform with sample-in-result-out characteristics has been developed for sensing ALP and BChE concurrently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and specificity of 3D origami μPAD-based fluorescent assay
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilic channel on the 1st layer can realize simultaneous detection of ALP and BChE with one sample injection. When drawing patterns on paper with oil-based marker, the ink can penetrate the entire paper. After the solvent evaporates quickly, the residual resin will form a hydrophobic barrier on the paper. By comparison with other μPAD fabricating methods, it does not require experience, large-scale instruments, additional power supplies, or professional skills. And the simple fabrication process can be completed by any non-technical person (Nie et al., 2012).

To obtain the optimal sensing of ALP and BChE activity, reaction parameters on 3D origami μPAD were optimized. As shown in Fig. S8, red ink solution flows from sample zone to reaction zone under the action of capillary force, indicating that a hydrophobic barrier can be successfully constructed by the commercial black oil-based marker, and the best sample volume is 20 μL. Taking these results as the gauge, Tris-HCl buffer (5 μL, 10 mM, pH 8.0) was dropped after folding two neighboring layers for layer-by-layer penetration and multi-step orderly reaction (Fig. S8b). Subsequently, reaction concentrations (PPi, 5 mM; BTCh, 0.1 M; Cu2+, 0.6 mM for ALP assay, 4 mM for BChE assay; OPD, 0.1 M; CDs, 3 mg mL−1 for ALP assay, 10 mg mL−1 for BChE assay), reaction time (enzyme and substrate, 30 min; substrate/enzymatic product and Cu2+, 10 min; Cu2+ and OPD, 25 min), reaction environment (Tris-HCl, 10 mM, 8.0), and reaction temperature (37 °C) were investigated and optimized on the 3D origami μPAD platform (Figs. S9–S11).

Biological interferences to the developed 3D Origami μPAD-based sensing platform were investigated to test the specificity, including ions, anions, amino acids, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and enzymes. As revealed in Fig. 3 a and b, negligible interference on ALP and BChE assay is found. The excellent selectivity of the developed platform makes practical applications a reality.

Under the optimized conditions, analytical merits (linearity, limit of detection (LOD), and reproducibility) for ALP and BChE activity detection were assessed. As depicted in Fig. 3c, with the increase of ALP activities, fluorescent images of detection layer gradually change from blue to yellow-green, and a good linear relationship between G/B and ALP activity in the range of 5.0–350.0 U L−1 is presented (y = 0.0032x + 0.9045, R 2 = 0.9875). LOD for ALP assay is estimated to be 3.6 U L−1 (3σ/S, σ is the standard deviation of blank samples and S is the slope of linear equation, n = 12). Contrary to the phenomenon of ALP sensing, fluorescent images of detection layer shift from yellow-green to blue with the increase of BChE activities (Fig. 3d). (R + G + B)/R shows a good linearity to the BChE activity in the range of 1.0–25.0 U mL−1 (y = 0.0817x + 2.2636, R
2 = 0.9931) with LOD at 0.4 U mL−1 (3σ/S). As was known, ALP and BChE levels in healthy human serum samples differ greatly, which are 53.0–130.0 U L−1 and 5.1–12.6 U mL−1 (Tong et al., 2020b). Notably, the linear range and LOD are enough to directly detect ALP and BChE simultaneously in real samples without any dilution step. Recently reported fluorescent methods for ALP and BChE detection were overviewed in Table S1. Obviously, almost all of them are single-enzyme assay approaches, and for BChE assay, real samples should be 1000-fold dilution. Thus, our developed platform had outstanding performances, such as simple and easy POCT, appropriate linear range, high selectivity, and simultaneous ratiometric detection.

In addition, reproducibility was evaluated by measuring ALP (25, 50, and 100 U L−1) and BChE (1, 5, and 10 U mL−1). Results indicated that the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of the intra-day assay (three parallel sensing) and inter-day assay (five-day span) are 1.2–4.9%, and 1.3–5.4%, which presents the acceptable reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real human serum sample assay by 3D origami μPAD platform
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the amenability of the proposed 3D origami μPAD platform for ALP and BChE detection, real human serum samples of healthy people and those with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) were analyzed without any pretreatment procedures. As illustrated in Table 1 , the test results agree well with those previously reported (Chen et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2019; Nechaeva et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2016). After spiking different concentrations of ALP and BChE, satisfactory recoveries (97.6–104.0%) with RSD less than 5.2% were achieved, indicating that the 3D origami μPAD platform is applicable for POCT of ALP and BChE in real samples with high accuracy.

In summary, for the first time, a ratiomeric fluorescent sensing-based 3D origami μPAD platform was constructed for simultaneous visual POCT of ALP and BChE. 3D origami μPAD was prepared easily by one-step plotting with commercially obtained black oil-based marker and specific metal molds without any professional skills or special equipment. Cascade catalytic reaction (enzyme and substrate, Cu2+ and OPD) was designed to successfully fabricate ratiometric fluorescent detection system with the same two fluorescent signal indicators (oxOPD and CDs), which can selectively and accurately sense ALP (linear range, 5.0–350.0 U L−1; LOD, 3.6 U L−1) and BChE (linear range, 1.0–25.0 U mL−1; LOD, 0.4 U mL−1). The developed 3D origami μPAD needs only 20 μL of real samples without any pretreatment procedures, and can be easily extended to detect other targets by simply changing biochemical reactions, which will find wide and excellent POCT applications in the future in different fields. And our further work will focus on simplifying assay steps to shorten analysis time.

Yongfeng Zhu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xia Tong: Conceptualization, Methodology. Qisheng Wei: Methodology, Investigation. Guihan Cai: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Yuanxin Cao: Formal analysis, Investigation. Chaoying Tong: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Writing-review. Shuyun Shi: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Fang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program, China (2019YFC1604904), Key R&D projects in Hunan Province, China, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University, China (2021zzts0067, 2021zzts0068).

ce:section.ce:para:
